intent: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

body.intent: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

draft.title: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments
draft.summary: Drafting your prototype from the swipes…
draft.html:
a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

Building your first draft…

data.intent: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

facade.hypothesis: Users prefer data displays with organic colors and soft gradients over clinical, high-contrast charts
facade.label: Warm data viz
facade.acceptImplies: Lean into natural palettes, rounded corners on graphs, subtle animations; avoid stark whites and neon accents
facade.rejectImplies: Explore clean minimalism, high contrast, sharp geometric charts, possibly darker backgrounds

evidence.hypothesis: Users prefer data displays with organic colors and soft gradients over clinical, high-contrast charts
evidence.implication: Lean into natural palettes, rounded corners on graphs, subtle animations; avoid stark whites and neon accents

facade.hypothesis: Users want to see health metrics update with fluid animations rather than instant number changes
facade.label: Smooth progress bars
facade.acceptImplies: Motion conveys data momentum; users find animated progress reinforcing and satisfying to watch
facade.rejectImplies: Users prefer quick, snappy feedback; animations feel slow or wasteful for tracking interface

facade.hypothesis: Users prefer a friendly, conversational voice over clinical/medical terminology when logging health experiments
facade.label: Casual check-in tone
facade.acceptImplies: Dashboard should use warm, approachable language—'How are you feeling today?' instead of 'Input vitals'—and encourage play/experimentation over strict medical framing
facade.rejectImplies: Users want a serious, credible, professional tone that signals this is a legitimate health tool, not a casual app—minimize personality, maximize clarity and authority

facade.hypothesis: Users benefit from persistent navigation (sidebar) paired with a flowing, vertically-scrollable card layout for experiments
facade.label: Sidebar with card stream
facade.acceptImplies: prioritize persistent sidebar, continuous scroll cards; good for quick scanning and adding experiments without nav re-entry
facade.rejectImplies: explore tabbed top nav or paginated views; suggests users want cleaner viewport or prefer grouped browsing over continuous feed

facade.hypothesis: Users want prominent metric displays (heart rate, steps, weight) without needing to read accompanying narrative or drill into detail
facade.axisTargeted: Dense data vs key metrics — testing whether the dashboard should lead with bold, scannable numbers or embed them in explanatory context
facade.label: Big numbers, small context
facade.acceptImplies: Build around large card-based gauges, KPI-style readouts, and at-a-glance numeric summaries; minimize text labels and explanatory copy
facade.rejectImplies: Users need narrative framing and context alongside numbers; design should pair metrics with mini-charts, trend arrows, or one-sentence summaries to make data meaningful

facade.hypothesis: Users tracking health experiments prefer soft, breathing whitespace and gentle visual hierarchy over dense information layouts
facade.label: Light, airy cards
facade.acceptImplies: Design with generous padding, minimal borders, lots of white space between elements—make the dashboard feel open and less clinical
facade.rejectImplies: Users want compact, information-dense layouts where more data is visible at once—prioritize efficiency over visual breathing room

axes.label: Whether user prefers minimal clinical aesthetics vs. warm, human-centered visual design for health data
Whether user wants data-dense dashboard layout vs. progressive disclosure with one metric at a time
axes.poleA: All at once or step by step?

axes.label: Whether user responds to motivational/encouraging tone vs. neutral, factual reporting voice
Whether user wants celebratory, gamified feedback vs. calm, meditative tracking environment
Whether user prefers raw numbers and charts vs. narrative summaries and insights
Whether user wants instant feedback on actions vs. slow, contemplative animations and transitions

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers minimal clinical aesthetics vs. warm, human-centered visual design for health data
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Iris

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants data-dense dashboard layout vs. progressive disclosure with one metric at a time
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Prism

scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Lumen

scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Aura

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers raw numbers and charts vs. narrative summaries and insights
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Facet

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants instant feedback on actions vs. slow, contemplative animations and transitions
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Echo

facade.hypothesis: Users find sparse, text-heavy layouts more trustworthy and less overwhelming than densely packed visualizations with many simultaneous metrics
facade.label: Minimal data grid
facade.acceptImplies: Design dashboard with generous whitespace, show 3-4 key metrics per view, use tables over complex charts, let users drill-down rather than overview-load
facade.rejectImplies: Users want rich visual density and overview-at-a-glance; favor multi-metric dashboards, heatmaps, and consolidated views that show many data points simultaneously

draft.title: Experiment Tracker Dashboard
draft.summary: A personal health dashboard for monitoring long-term experiments with visual progress tracking, experiment cards, and key metrics at a glance.
